nderstood that he meant a great deal more than that. In conseque
nce, I'm inclined to reserve all judgments, a habit that has opened up many curious natures to me and also made me the victim of not a few veteran bores. The ab
normal mind is quick to detect and attach itself to this quality when it appears in a normal person, and so it came about that in college I was unjustly accused
 of being a politician, because I was privy to the secret griefs of wild, unknown men. Most of the confidences were unsought---frequently I have feigned sleep,
preoccupation, or a hostile levity when I realized by some unmistakable sign that an intimate revelation was quivering on the horizon; for the intimate revelati
ons of young men, or at least the terms in which they express them, are usually plagiaristic and marred by obvious suppressions. Reserving judgments is a matter
 of infinite hope. I am still a little afraid of missing something if I forget that, as my father snobbishly suggested, and I snobbishly repeat, a sense of the
fundamental decencies is parcelled out unequally at birth.

And, after boasting this way of my tolerance, I come to the admission that it has a limit. Conduct may be founded on the hard rock or the wet marshes, but after
 a certain point I don't care what it's founded on. When I came back from the East last autumn I felt that I wanted the world to be in uniform and at a sort of
moral attention forever; I wanted no more riotous excursions with privileged glimpses into the human heart. Only Gatsby, the man who gives his name to this book
, was exempt from my reaction---Gatsby, who represented everything for which I have an unaffected scorn. If personality is an unbroken series of successful gest
ures, then there was something gorgeous about him, some heightened sensitivity to the promises of life, as if he were related to one of those intricate machines
 that register earthquakes ten thousand miles away. This responsiveness had nothing to do with that flabby impressionability which is dignified under the name o
f the ``creative temperament''---it was an extraordinary gift for hope, a romantic readiness such as I have never found in any other person and which it is not
likely I shall ever find again. No---Gatsby turned out all right at the end; it is what preyed on Gatsby, what foul dust floated in the wake of his dreams that
temporarily closed out my interest in the abortive sorrows and short-winded elations of men.

My family have been prominent, well-to-do people in this Middle Western city for three generations. The Carraways are something of a clan, and we have a traditi
on that we're descended from the Dukes of Buccleuch, but the actual founder of my line was my grandfather's brother, who came here in fifty-one, sent a substitu
te to the Civil War, and started the wholesale hardware business that my father carries on to-day.

I never saw this great-uncle, but I'm supposed to look like him---with special reference to the rather hard-boiled painting that hangs in father's office. I gra
duated from New Haven in 1915, just a quarter of a century after my father, and a little later I participated in that delayed Teutonic migration known as the Gr
eat War. I enjoyed the counter-raid so thoroughly that I came back restless. Instead of being the warm centre of the world, the Middle West now seemed like the
ragged edge of the universe---so I decided to go East and learn the bond business. Everybody I knew was in the bond business, so I supposed it could support one
 more single man. All my aunts and uncles talked it over as if they were choosing a prep school for me, and finally said, ``Why---ye-es,'' with very grave, hesi
tant faces. Father agreed to finance me for a year, and after various delays I came East, permanently, I thought, in the spring of twenty-two.

The practical thing was to find rooms in the city, but it was a warm season, and I had just left a country of wide lawns and friendly trees, so when a young man
 at the office suggested that we take a house together in a commuting town, it sounded like a great idea. He found the house, a weatherbeaten cardboard bungalow
 at eighty a month, but at the last minute the firm ordered him to Washington, and I went out to the country alone. I had a dog---at least I had him for a few d
ays until he ran away---and an old Dodge and a Finnish woman, who made my bed and cooked breakfast and muttered Finnish wisdom to herself over the electric stov
e.

It was lonely for a day or so until one morning some man, more recently arrived than I, stopped me on the road.

``How do you get to West Egg village?'' he asked helplessly.

I told him. And as I walked on I was lonely no longer. I was a guide, a pathfinder, an original settler. He had casually conferred on me the freedom of the neig
hborhood.

{
  "analysis": "Great. Synonyms mapping works. Now we need to process each overfull hbox. Let's write a Python script to do the replacements automatically. We'll load input.tex, find words in synonym families, and for each overfull line, we can attempt to replace a word with a shorter synonym. However, we need to know which exact line in the compiled PDF corresponds to the overfull hbox; we have only a snippet. We could approximate by searching for the snippet in the text. Let's write a script that does the following:
1. Parse the log file to extract overfull hbox snippets (the line after each Overfull).
2. For each snippet, find its location in input.tex.
3. Identify the words in that snippet that are in synonym families.
4. Choose a synonym that is shorter (or longer) to reduce the overfull width. Since we have the overfull width in points, we can estimate needed reduction in characters (assuming average char width). We'll just try to replace with the shortest synonym available.
5. Replace the word in the text (case-insensitive, but keep original case? The synonyms are lowercased; we need to preserve capitalization. We'll match case-insensitively and replace with same case pattern: if original word is capitalized, capitalize the synonym; if all caps, all caps; else lowercase.
6. After replacements, write new input.tex.
7. Recompile and check for overfull hboxes.
8. Iterate until none remain.
Let's create the script now.",
  "plan": "Create a Python script 'fix_overfull.py' in /app. Then run it, compile, and see results. We'll need to be careful to only edit input.tex. Let's start by writing the script.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "cd /app
",
      "duration": 0.1
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "cat > fix_overfull.py << 'EOF'
import re
import sys

# Load synonyms
synonyms = {}
with open('synonyms.txt') as f:
    for line in f:
        line = line.strip()
        if not line:
            continue
        words = [w.strip() for w in line.split(',')]
        for w in words:
            synonyms[w.lower()] = words

# Load input text
with open('input.tex') as f:
    text = f.read()

def find_word_positions(text):
    """Return dict mapping (start, end) to word and lower version"""
    positions = {}
    for m in re.finditer(r'\b([A-Za-z\-\']+)\b', text):
        word = m.group(1)
        start, end = m.start(1), m.end(1)
        positions[(start, end)] = word
    return positions

def case_transform(original, replacement):
    """Apply same case pattern as original to replacement."""
    if original.isupper():
        return replacement.upper()
    elif original[0].isupper() and original[1:].islower():
        return replacement.capitalize()
    else:
        return replacement.lower()

def replace_word(text, start, end, new_word):
    return text[:start] + new_word + text[end:]

# Parse log for overfull snippets
log_lines = []
with open('main.log') as f:
    lines = f.readlines()
    i = 0
    while i < len(lines):
        if 'Overfull' in lines[i]:
            # next line is snippet
            if i+1 < len(lines):
                snippet = lines[i+1].strip()
                # extract only the text after the font spec
                # pattern: \OT1/.../.../10 <text>
                m = re.search(r'\\OT1/[^/]+/[^/]+/[^/]+/\d+\s+(.*)', snippet)
                if m:
                    snippet_text = m.group(1)
                    # remove hyphenation hyphens? they are just for display
                    snippet_text = re.sub(r'-', '', snippet_text)
                    log_lines.append(snippet_text)
            i += 2
        else:
            i += 1

print('Found overfull snippets:', log_lines)

# For each snippet, locate in text
for snippet in log_lines:
    # snippet may be partial; we need to find the exact location.
    # Use regex to find the snippet with hyphens removed
    snippet_clean = re.sub(r'[^a-zA-Z\s.\-]', '', snippet)  # keep letters, spaces, dots, hyphens
    # we'll just search for the first few words
    words = snippet_clean.split()
    # take first three words
    if len(words) >= 3:
        search_term = ' '.join(words[:3])
    else:
        search_term = ' '.join(words)
    # remove hyphens
    search_term = search_term.replace('-', '')
    # find in text (case-insensitive)
    idx = text.lower().find(search_term.lower())
    if idx == -1:
        print('Could not find:', search_term)
        continue
    # get surrounding text to identify exact word boundaries
    # we'll look at positions around idx
    window = text[max(0, idx-50):min(len(text), idx+150)]
    print('Found snippet in window:', window[:100])
    # Now we need to decide which word to replace.
    # For simplicity, replace the longest word that has synonyms.
    # Extract words in window
    word_matches = list(re.finditer(r'\b([A-Za-z\-\']+)\b', window))
    candidates = []
    for m in word_matches:
        w = m.group(1)
        w_low = w.lower()
        if w_low in synonyms:
            # find shortest synonym
            syns = synonyms[w_low]
            shortest = min(syns, key=len)
            if len(shortest) < len(w_low):
                candidates.append((m.start(), m.end(), w, shortest))
    if candidates:
        # choose candidate with largest length reduction
        candidates.sort(key=lambda x: len(x[2]) - len(x[3]), reverse=True)
        start_global = idx - 50 + candidates[0][0] if idx-50 >=0 else idx + candidates[0][0]
        end_global = start_global + len(candidates[0][2])
        new_word = case_transform(candidates[0][2], candidates[0][3])
        print(f'Replacing "{candidates[0][2]}" with "{new_word}"')
        text = replace_word(text, start_global, end_global, new_word)
    else:
        print('No replaceable word found for snippet:', snippet)

# Write back
with open('input.tex', 'w') as f:
    f.write(text)
print('Updated input.tex')
